name: dead-code-survives-task-completion
tags: dead code, task hygiene, task status, superseded implementation, cleanup, orphaned function, task:c05204ac
body:
When a later task/refactor supersedes an earlier task's implementation (e.g. moving logic from a bare function into a properly-logged dispatcher handler), the old function can be left behind as dead code even after the newer code is confirmed working in production logs — and the original task can stay open/pending indefinitely since nobody circles back to close it once the goal is de facto met elsewhere.

Why: found via this exact pattern in claude-hooks-dev: hooks/server.py had a bare `_evict_session()` (no dispatcher logging) sitting unreferenced since before task c05204ac, while the actual live route called dispatcher._handle_session_end — which fulfilled c05204ac's stated goal (SessionEnd eviction + logging) as an incidental side effect of a different task (b7cb4eb4/c2a8f350). The task was never marked done and the dead function was never deleted.

How to apply: when investigating "who does X" or "is this still used" during a debugging/exploration session, grep for the function name across the repo before trusting a docstring or an open task's description — an open task doesn't mean the work wasn't done, and a documented function doesn't mean it's live. Cross-check open tasks whose described goal matches code you've already found working elsewhere; they're often stale-open rather than actually blocked.
